notes.anchor: that Calvena takes it badly that Brutus suspects him
notes.note: "Calvena" ("Baldy") is Cicero's mocking nickname for Gaius Matius, Caesar's loyal friend; Matius was offended that Brutus distrusted him for his continuing devotion to the dead dictator's memory.

notes.anchor: if it is with the standards that the legions are coming from Gaul
notes.note: Cicero plays on signa, both "signs/omens" and military "standards": pretty omens, but no good omen if the Gallic legions march on Italy under their standards (i.e. ready for war).

notes.anchor: And what of those Annius shipped over
notes.note: An "Annius" (probably T. Annius Cimber, an Antonian partisan) who had ferried legions across to Italy; no contemporary Annius could be matched to a registry entity, so left unbound and flagged.

notes.anchor: I meant Gaius Asinius --- but a [Greek: mnemonikon hamartema], a slip of memory
notes.note: Gaius Asinius Pollio, the Caesarian commander then in Further Spain (and later the historian and patron); Cicero, correcting himself in Greek (mnemonikon hamartema, "a memory-slip"), had first named the wrong man.
Caesar's wealthy and powerful freedmen (imperial-style household agents) were rumoured to be intriguing; Cicero thinks Antony could easily crush them if he chose.

notes.anchor: you see, all the same, the tyrant's hangers-on holding commands
notes.note: The "tyrant" is the assassinated Caesar; Cicero laments that Caesar's partisans still hold the provincial commands, armies, and the loyalty of the veterans, leaving the Liberators honoured but powerless.

notes.anchor: all things [Greek: euripista], easily blown about
notes.note: Greek euripista, "shifting like the Euripus" (the notoriously fickle tidal strait between Euboea and the mainland); Cicero's figure for how unreliable and changeable the veterans' loyalty is.

notes.anchor: But I should like to know about Octavius's arrival
notes.note: The eighteen-year-old Gaius Octavius, Caesar's grand-nephew and adopted heir (the future Augustus), had just landed in Italy; Cicero watches anxiously whether crowds and troops will rally to him --- the first sign of his political emergence.

notes.anchor: on the third day before the Ides
notes.note: A Roman date: counted inclusively backward from the Ides (the 13th of April here), giving the 11th of April 44 BC; Romans dated by counting toward the Kalends, Nones, and Ides rather than forward from the month's start.
